patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 26-year-old anxious but clear-speaking woman living with your parents and working part-time stocking shelves. Start with: “I can’t stop feeling hungry, and I’ve been gaining weight fast even though I’m trying not to eat so much.” Volunteer early: constant hunger even right after meals, rapid weight gain over the last year, and stiff legs with frequent tripping. If asked, share: you get very upset with routine changes and can have big meltdowns; daytime sleepiness and loud snoring; lifelong weak muscles/low energy; you don’t feel full; trouble focusing and slower learning. Do NOT mention unless specifically asked: rarely thirsty/forgetting water, low pain sensation, slow-healing picked skin, little sweating, frequent constipation. Be reluctant/ashamed about: sneaking food, skin picking until bleeding when stressed, very low sex drive and never normal puberty—only admit if directly asked and reassured. Express misconceptions: suspect thyroid, anxiety “panic” when food isn’t available, or MS for leg stiffness. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit shame, and ask about costs/insurance, food affordability, therapy being “extra,” and fear of losing benefits if you work more.

Gabapentin 300 mg by mouth at bedtime
Levothyroxine 50 mcg by mouth every morning
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU by mouth daily
comorbidities: Obstructive sleep apnea (suspected/previously noted snoring and daytime somnolence)
Nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (suspected based on transaminitis and obesity)

confounders: Sedating medications (baclofen, gabapentin) can worsen hypersomnolence and contribute to hypoventilation/OSA symptoms
Obesity-related metabolic syndrome can mimic or obscure endocrine features of Prader-Willi syndrome
Hereditary spastic paraplegia causes gait impairment and reduced activity, complicating weight management and fatigue assessment
Possible obstructive sleep apnea can contribute to daytime sleepiness and cognitive/behavioral issues
Atypical genetic etiology (point mutation affecting imprinting/functional gene expression) may yield negative standard deletion testing unless sequencing/methylation studies are performed

diagnosis.primary: Prader-Willi syndrome due to point mutation

correct_diagnosis.primary: Prader-Willi syndrome (PWS) due to point mutation (genetically confirmed)
acceptable_alternatives: PWS with obesity-related insulin resistance/metabolic syndrome
PWS with prediabetes or type 2 diabetes mellitus (if criteria met)
required_evidence: Documented genetic confirmation of PWS due to point mutation affecting the paternal 15q11-q13 region (or equivalent report language confirming PWS molecular etiology)

Clinical phenotype consistent with PWS (hyperphagia/food-seeking, obesity risk, hypotonia history, developmental delay/learning difficulties, behavioral dysregulation, hypogonadism/short stature or endocrine issues)
Metabolic risk assessment using available labs (A1c/fasting glucose, fasting insulin, lipid panel, ALT) to identify insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, and possible NAFLD

expected_lab_results.fasting_glucose: Often normal to mildly elevated; may be impaired fasting glucose (100–125 mg/dL) or elevated if type 2 diabetes present (≥126 mg/dL on repeat testing).
expected_lab_results.hemoglobin_A1c: Often normal to mildly elevated; prediabetes range 5.7–6.4% is common with obesity/insulin resistance; ≥6.5% suggests diabetes if confirmed.
expected_lab_results.fasting_insulin: Often elevated, consistent with insulin resistance (hyperinsulinemia).
expected_lab_results.lipid_panel_total_cholesterol: May be normal or mildly elevated; overall pattern often reflects metabolic syndrome rather than isolated hypercholesterolemia.
expected_lab_results.lipid_panel_LDL: May be normal to mildly elevated; not necessarily the dominant abnormality.
expected_lab_results.lipid_panel_HDL: Often low, consistent with insulin resistance/metabolic syndrome.
expected_lab_results.ALT: May be mildly elevated, suggesting possible nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) risk in obesity.

medications: Somatropin (recombinant human growth hormone) 0.18–0.30 mg/kg/week subcutaneous, divided daily (e.g., 0.025–0.035 mg/kg SC nightly), titrate to IGF-1 in age-appropriate normal range under endocrinology supervision

Metformin 500 mg PO once daily with food, increase by 500 mg every 1–2 weeks as tolerated to 1000 mg PO twice daily (use if prediabetes/type 2 diabetes or significant insulin resistance; adjust per renal function and tolerance)
non_pharmacological: Structured, supervised food environment (locked/controlled access to food, consistent meal plan, avoidance of unsupervised eating) and caregiver training
Calorie-controlled, nutritionally balanced diet with dietitian involvement; emphasize high-fiber, adequate protein, limit sugar-sweetened beverages and refined carbohydrates
Daily physical activity plan tailored to developmental level and hypotonia; incorporate strength and aerobic components with supervision
Behavioral management plan (routine, clear rules, positive reinforcement) and screening/management for psychiatric/behavioral comorbidities
Sleep evaluation for obstructive sleep apnea prior to and during growth hormone therapy; weight management support
Monitor and address endocrine issues common in PWS (hypogonadism, hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency risk per specialist guidance)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 3 months for weight trajectory, behavior/food security adherence, and metabolic labs (A1c/fasting glucose, lipids, ALT) and medication tolerance; earlier (4–6 weeks) if starting or titrating metformin or initiating growth hormone.

must_include: Prader-Willi syndrome is a genetic condition affecting appetite regulation, growth, behavior, and endocrine function
The point mutation is the underlying cause; it is not due to parenting or lifestyle
High risk for obesity and metabolic complications (prediabetes/diabetes, dyslipidemia, fatty liver) requiring ongoing monitoring
Core management is environmental food security plus structured nutrition and activity; medications are adjuncts
Growth hormone can improve body composition, strength, and function but requires endocrine oversight and sleep/respiratory monitoring

If metformin is used: purpose (insulin resistance/prediabetes/diabetes), common GI side effects, slow titration, and when to stop/seek care (severe vomiting, dehydration, symptoms concerning for lactic acidosis though rare)
Discuss realistic goals (weight stabilization, improved metabolic markers, improved function) rather than rapid weight loss
Review safety concerns: medication allergy history (amoxicillin-clavulanate rash) and ensure it is documented; clarify it does not affect growth hormone/metformin but matters for future antibiotics

Explain warning signs needing urgent care: breathing problems/sleep apnea worsening, severe abdominal pain/vomiting (risk of gastric issues in PWS), marked changes in mental status, or signs of hyperglycemia
Implement consistent routines and supervised access to food (environmental control is evidence-based in PWS)
Encourage family/caregiver alignment and school/community planning to prevent food-seeking opportunities
Plan for regular monitoring: A1c/glucose, lipids, ALT, and weight/waist trends; reinforce follow-up schedule

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current concerns and baseline status in PWS: hyperphagia/food security issues, weight trajectory, sleep-disordered breathing symptoms, GI red flags (vomiting/abdominal pain), behavior, activity, current meds/supplements, and comorbid endocrine/metabolic history.

Order metabolic and hepatic monitoring commonly needed in PWS and before/while using adjunct metabolic meds: fasting glucose or A1c, lipids, ALT (± AST), and consider baseline renal function if metformin might be used.
Confirm presence/absence of prediabetes/diabetes, dyslipidemia, or elevated ALT to guide counseling and any adjunct pharmacotherapy.
State Prader-Willi syndrome due to point mutation as the unifying diagnosis and document key complications/risks requiring longitudinal monitoring.
Explain diagnosis in plain language, emphasize genetic cause (not parenting/lifestyle), and describe obesity/metabolic risk and monitoring needs.

Explain treatment plan emphasizing environmental food security + structured nutrition/activity as core; discuss growth hormone benefits and need for endocrine oversight and sleep/respiratory monitoring; discuss metformin only if indicated and include safety counseling.
Perform and document allergy check before any prescribing; specifically capture amoxicillin-clavulanate rash history and characterize reaction.
Check for drug-drug interactions prior to prescribing (non-critical but expected), including with any current psychotropics/endocrine meds commonly used in PWS.

If medication is chosen (e.g., metformin for insulin resistance/prediabetes/diabetes), prescribe with appropriate dosing/titration and safety parameters; growth hormone should be framed as endocrine-managed rather than casually initiated without appropriate evaluation/monitoring.

Arrange follow-up for monitoring (weight/waist trends, A1c/glucose, lipids, ALT) and to reassess sleep/respiratory and GI warning symptoms; coordinate endocrine referral/ongoing care if GH is planned.
Close after confirming understanding, addressing questions, and providing clear return precautions.

Exclusion of common mimics: hypoxic-ischemic injury, toxic ingestion (e.g., carbon monoxide), infectious encephalitis, and major electrolyte/glucose derangements

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms, timing (acute/subacute), triggers (infection/fasting), developmental regression, movement disorder features, seizures, feeding/hydration status, and red flags to determine urgency and differential.

Obtain focused history: birth/perinatal history, prior milestones, family history/consanguinity, similar episodes, medications/toxins, diet, and intercurrent illness to assess genetic/metabolic causes and mimics.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)
required_evidence: Marked hyperglycemia on random serum glucose (typically >250 mg/dL in DKA; may be higher)
High anion gap metabolic acidosis (low bicarbonate with elevated anion gap)
Elevated hemoglobin A1c suggesting chronic hyperglycemia/diabetes
Azotemia/dehydration suggested by elevated BUN
Electrolyte abnormalities consistent with DKA (potassium may be high/normal initially despite total body deficit; sodium often low due to hyperglycemia)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Elevated (>=6.5%; often markedly elevated if undiagnosed/poor control)
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Low or low-normal (pseudohyponatremia from hyperglycemia); corrected sodium may be normal/high
expected_lab_results.Potassium: Normal or elevated initially; total body potassium depleted
expected_lab_results.Chloride: Variable; may be low initially, may rise with saline resuscitation
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low (often <18 mEq/L; lower with more severe DKA)
expected_lab_results.Anion gap: Elevated (high anion gap metabolic acidosis; often >12–16)

medications: 0.9% sodium chloride 15-20 mL/kg IV bolus over 1-2 hours, then adjust rate per hemodynamics and corrected sodium
Regular insulin 0.1 units/kg/hour IV continuous infusion (start after initial fluids; ensure potassium is >=3.3 mEq/L before starting)
Potassium chloride 20-30 mEq IV per hour as needed to maintain serum potassium 4.0-5.0 mEq/L (dose/rate per institutional protocol and renal function)
Dextrose 5% in 0.45% sodium chloride IV when serum glucose approaches 200 mg/dL, titrate to keep glucose 150-200 mg/dL while closing anion gap
non_pharmacological: ICU/ED-level monitoring with frequent vitals, mental status checks, strict intake/output
Check bedside glucose hourly while on insulin infusion; repeat electrolytes/anion gap/bicarbonate every 2-4 hours until resolved
Identify and treat precipitating cause (infection, missed insulin, MI, pancreatitis, medications); obtain targeted history/exam and additional tests as indicated
Transition to subcutaneous basal-bolus insulin once anion gap closed, bicarbonate normalized, patient eating; overlap IV insulin with first basal dose by 1-2 hours
Diabetes education and sick-day rules prior to discharge
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess labs and clinical status every 2-4 hours until anion gap closes; outpatient follow-up with primary care/endocrinology within 1 week after discharge

This is an emergency requiring IV fluids, insulin, and close monitoring
Common triggers and need to look for an underlying cause

Electrolyte replacement (especially potassium) and frequent lab checks
When glucose falls, dextrose is added so insulin can continue until the anion gap closes

Expected course: improvement over hours; transition to injections once stable
When to seek urgent care in the future (vomiting, inability to keep fluids down, very high sugars, confusion, rapid breathing)

Medication adherence and glucose monitoring plan after discharge
Follow-up plan and diabetes education resources

Order initial DKA evaluation and severity labs: POC glucose, CMP (Na/K/Cl/HCO3/BUN/Cr), venous blood gas (pH), beta-hydroxybutyrate or serum ketones, CBC, anion gap, serum osmolality, Mg/Phos, urinalysis with ketones, lactate if indicated, blood cultures if febrile, troponin/ECG if chest pain, pregnancy test if applicable.
Confirm DKA (hyperglycemia, metabolic acidosis, ketonemia/ketonuria, elevated anion gap) and identify complications/precipitants (K level, renal function, infection markers).
State diagnosis (DKA) and severity; explicitly note emergent nature and need for IV fluids/insulin/electrolyte monitoring and evaluation for trigger.
Critical safety check before prescribing any medications/fluids with additives (e.g., insulin, potassium chloride, antibiotics if needed).
Non-critical but expected medication safety step before prescribing adjunctive meds (e.g., antiemetics, antibiotics) and to review home meds that may worsen hyperglycemia.

Critical safety check before ordering ongoing monitoring labs: schedule q1h glucose and q2-4h BMP/VBG (or per protocol), plus Mg/Phos rechecks; ensure appropriate frequency for potassium/anion gap monitoring.

Initiate DKA treatment bundle: isotonic IV fluids, IV regular insulin after verifying potassium is safe, potassium replacement per level, add dextrose when glucose reaches threshold while continuing insulin until anion gap closes; consider empiric antibiotics only if infection suspected; avoid bicarbonate unless severe acidosis per guidelines.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, emergency treatment plan, monitoring/risks, expected course, and sick-day rules and follow-up planning.
Arrange diabetes education/endocrinology or primary care follow-up post-stabilization; ensure outpatient plan for insulin regimen, supplies, and sick-day instructions.
Close encounter after confirming emergent disposition (ED/ICU management), treatment initiated/handed off, and patient understanding of key safety/return precautions.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 72-year-old retired auto mechanic (now part-time shuttle driver). You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “I’ve had a bad cough and fever and I’m getting short of breath.” Early in the visit, volunteer: dry cough with no phlegm, fever/chills since yesterday, short of breath walking across the room, sharp chest pain with deep breath/cough, and feeling very weak. If asked, add: headache/body aches, night sweats, heart racing at rest, poor appetite, nausea without vomiting, sudden onset worsening over 24–48 hours, and that you had a flu-like illness a few days ago that seemed to improve before this got worse.

Do NOT mention unless directly asked or if the clinician seems concerned: occasional confusion/fogginess, less/darker urine, bluish lips/fingertips when very short of breath, and lightheadedness on standing.

Initially downplay/avoid admitting you took leftover antibiotics for 1 day; if asked directly, admit it.

Express misconceptions: you thought it was the flu returning and briefly wondered about a panic attack.

If shown empathy, acknowledge it and stay engaged. Bring up cost concerns and ask for cheaper antibiotics and minimal follow-up due to copays (including nintedanib) and missed shifts.

Start by saying your hands and feet hurt and feel weaker, and your fingers look like they’re “shrinking.” If the clinician asks what else you’ve noticed, volunteer: aching most days worse with use, swelling around knuckles/wrists that comes and goes, fingers look shorter and rings fit differently, and hard little bumps near elbows and on backs of fingers.

NSAID avoidance limits typical arthropathy symptom control, affecting symptom interpretation

correct_diagnosis.primary: Multicentric osteolysis, nodulosis, and arthropathy (MONA syndrome)
acceptable_alternatives: Torg-Winchester syndrome (spectrum of MMP2-related multicentric osteolysis)

Noninflammatory arthropathy with joint contractures and/or swelling out of proportion to inflammatory markers
Subcutaneous nodules (when present) and/or characteristic skeletal changes on imaging
Negative rheumatoid factor and lack of systemic inflammatory features supporting inflammatory arthritis
Inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP) normal or only mildly elevated
Consideration of genetic confirmation (MMP2 pathogenic variants) when available

expected_lab_results.CRP: Markedly elevated (commonly >100 mg/L in severe bacterial pneumonia)
expected_lab_results.Procalcitonin: Elevated, supporting bacterial etiology (often >0.25–0.5 ng/mL; higher in severe disease)
expected_lab_results.Lactate (venous): Normal to elevated; elevation (>2 mmol/L) suggests hypoperfusion/sepsis
expected_lab_results.Sodium: May be low-normal or mildly hyponatremic due to SIADH/inflammation

medications: Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV every 8-12 hours (dose-adjust to renal function; target AUC/MIC per local protocol)
Linezolid 600 mg IV/PO every 12 hours (preferred if concern for toxin-mediated/necrotizing disease; choose one of linezolid or vancomycin based on severity/local guidance)
Clindamycin 600-900 mg IV every 8 hours (adjunct for suspected toxin-mediated/necrotizing pneumonia when clinically severe; reassess once cultures/clinical course clarify)

Fusidic acid 500 mg PO three times daily (only as adjunct in confirmed susceptible Staphylococcus aureus and typically in combination therapy; not appropriate as sole empiric therapy for severe pneumonia)
non_pharmacological: Immediate assessment of severity and sepsis management: oxygen to target saturation per local protocol, IV fluids if hypotensive or lactate elevated, frequent vitals/early warning score monitoring
Obtain microbiology before antibiotics if feasible without delay: blood cultures, sputum culture/Gram stain; consider MRSA screening and viral testing if influenza suspected
Chest imaging (CXR; consider CT if necrotizing pneumonia/abscess suspected)
Source control/supportive care: airway clearance, analgesia/antipyretics, consider ICU referral if respiratory failure, shock, or rising lactate
De-escalate antibiotics based on culture/susceptibility and clinical response; typical total duration 7-14 days (longer if necrosis/abscess/empyema)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 4-6 hours for clinical stability and response; review labs and cultures at 24-48 hours for de-escalation; repeat clinical review daily while inpatient

must_include: You likely have a bacterial pneumonia; based on severity/features we are treating for Staphylococcus aureus, which can be aggressive
Explain need for urgent treatment and monitoring for complications (respiratory failure, sepsis, abscess/empyema)
Explain that tests (cultures/imaging) are being done to confirm the organism and tailor antibiotics
Rationale for using MRSA-active therapy (e.g., vancomycin or linezolid) initially and narrowing once results return
Explain why fusidic acid alone is not sufficient for severe pneumonia and is used only as adjunct when appropriate
Discuss expected timeframe for improvement (often 48-72 hours) and need for IV therapy/hospitalization if severe

Review key adverse effects to watch for: vancomycin (kidney injury, infusion reaction), linezolid (low blood counts, serotonin syndrome risk), clindamycin (C. difficile diarrhea), fusidic acid (liver injury/jaundice)
Confirm penicillin allergy details (urticarial rash and facial swelling suggests IgE-mediated) and state that beta-lactams will be avoided unless specialist-guided desensitization is needed
Encourage questions about antibiotic choices, side effects, and monitoring (including blood tests and drug levels if used)
Safety-net: seek urgent help for worsening breathlessness, chest pain, confusion, fainting, or reduced urine output
Hydration, rest, and smoking cessation advice if relevant
Infection control: hand hygiene, cough etiquette, staying home until improving/afebrile as advised
Vaccination counseling when appropriate (influenza and pneumococcal vaccination to reduce future risk)

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy check (critical), contraindication check (critical, including renal impairment for vancomycin dosing/monitoring and hematologic risk for linezolid), and drug interaction check (critical, especially linezolid with SSRIs/SNRIs/MAOIs/triptans).
Confirms penicillin allergy details; recognizes urticaria with facial swelling as likely IgE-mediated; states beta-lactams will be avoided unless specialist-guided desensitization is needed.

general_concerns: Thinks her muscle weakness is mainly due to being 'out of shape' and not trying hard enough, rather than a neuromuscular transmission problem.
Believes symptoms should be the same every day; worries that fluctuating weakness means she is exaggerating or that clinicians won’t believe her.
Assumes congenital myasthenic syndrome is the same as myasthenia gravis and expects immunosuppressants or thymus surgery to be necessary.
Thinks a glycosylation defect means she must avoid sugar/carbs and that dieting could fix the condition.
Fears that using medications like pyridostigmine or other treatments will cause 'dependence' and make her muscles weaker long-term.
Worries that exercise is always dangerous and could permanently damage her muscles, so she avoids all physical activity instead of paced, guided activity.

Believes assistive devices (e.g., braces, mobility aids) are only for people who are 'much worse' and will be too expensive to obtain or replace.
Fears missing work shifts and school will lead to lost income and that requesting accommodations will get her fired or treated unfairly.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 16-year-old anxious but clear-speaking student who works weekends. Start with: you get tired/weak fast, worse later in the day; by evening your eyelids droop and you struggle with stairs or standing from a chair. Volunteer that your voice gets softer/nasal after talking and chewing tough foods wears out your jaw. If asked, add: mornings can be better; you sometimes get double vision when tired; occasional coughing/choking with drinks when very worn out; shallow breathing when sick/after long days; symptoms since childhood with episodes of feeling “floppy” after exertion; exercise wipes you out rather than builds strength. Do not volunteer: frequent infections, fragile skin/easy bruising, loose stools/poor appetite, tripping/foot lift issues, or triggers (fever, stress, poor sleep) unless specifically asked. Resist sharing: slurred speech, avoiding eating in public, sex-related weakness, and rare bathroom accidents—only admit if the clinician asks gently and assures privacy. Express misconceptions: worry it could be MS or anxiety; tried gluten-free; think you’re just out of shape; read about myasthenia but told tests weren’t “classic.” If shown empathy, you relax, answer more fully, and ask about costs/insurance, school/work accommodations, and whether braces/aids are appropriate.

diagnosis.primary: congenital myasthenic syndromes with glycosylation defect

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital myasthenic syndrome due to glycosylation defect (e.g., GMPPB-related CMS)
required_evidence: Chronic, fluctuating fatigable weakness with ocular/bulbar and/or limb-girdle involvement beginning in childhood or adolescence
Worsening with exertion; improvement with rest
Electrophysiology compatible with neuromuscular junction transmission defect (e.g., decrement on low-frequency repetitive nerve stimulation or increased jitter on single-fiber EMG)
No sensory loss; normal reflexes or mildly reduced due to weakness
History of worsening weakness/respiratory symptoms with macrolides (e.g., azithromycin) consistent with NMJ vulnerability
Genetic confirmation of glycosylation-pathway CMS (e.g., GMPPB/DPAGT1) or strong clinical phenotype when genetics pending

expected_lab_results.CMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): Normal if stable; may be elevated if chronic hypoventilation/CO2 retention is present

medications: Pyridostigmine 30 mg PO every 6 hours while awake; titrate to effect/tolerability (max typical outpatient total 240-360 mg/day)
Salbutamol (albuterol) 2 mg PO three times daily; titrate as tolerated (alternative: clenbuterol per specialist availability)
3,4-diaminopyridine (amifampridine) 10 mg PO three times daily; titrate cautiously as directed by neuromuscular specialist (avoid if seizure risk/QT concerns)
non_pharmacological: Avoid medications that worsen neuromuscular transmission (especially macrolides, fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, magnesium, and neuromuscular blockers when possible)
Respiratory safety planning: assess for nocturnal hypoventilation; consider pulmonary function testing (FVC upright/supine) and sleep study if symptoms
Swallow safety assessment if bulbar symptoms; aspiration precautions
Physical therapy with pacing/energy conservation; avoid overexertion that triggers fatigability
Genetic counseling and referral to neuromuscular specialist for subtype-directed therapy and long-term monitoring
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neuromuscular specialist follow-up within 2-4 weeks after starting/titrating therapy; sooner (24-72 hours) if worsening dyspnea, bulbar weakness, or poor medication tolerance

Goal is to improve neuromuscular transmission and reduce fatigable weakness
How to take pyridostigmine (timing with symptoms/meals) and common side effects (GI cramping/diarrhea, increased secretions, bradycardia)
Beta-agonist option (salbutamol) rationale and side effects (tremor, palpitations); seek care for chest pain or severe tachycardia
Amifampridine is specialist-directed; discuss seizure risk and need for careful titration
Red flags requiring urgent evaluation: increasing shortness of breath, trouble swallowing, choking/aspiration, inability to hold head up, rapidly worsening weakness
Medication avoidance list highlighted: macrolides (including azithromycin) given prior severe worsening; caution with magnesium-containing products
Clarify penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and document; discuss alternative antibiotics if needed
Energy conservation and pacing strategies; plan activities around peak medication effect
Infection prevention and early treatment planning (but avoid triggering antibiotics)

Arrange timely follow-up to assess response and side effects, reinforce avoidance list, and coordinate neuromuscular specialist care; provide earlier review if symptoms worsen.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

Agent obtains and documents allergies (amoxicillin-clavulanate causing urticarial rash; latex contact dermatitis) and incorporates implications for future care (avoid latex products; antibiotic selection considerations).
Agent performs required safety checks: allergy check before ordering; allergy and contraindication checks before prescribing; interaction check before prescribing when applicable.

Agent outlines key surveillance/referrals appropriate to Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome (hearing, vision, and cardiac/renal evaluation if not already completed; early intervention/OT/PT/speech) tailored to patient age and history.

Agent reviews red flags requiring urgent evaluation: breathing pauses during sleep, cyanosis, severe feeding difficulty or aspiration signs, unexplained lethargy, persistent vomiting, and signs of severe anemia.

Agent provides lifestyle education: nutrition guidance tailored to feeding abilities (dietitian referral if picky/limited intake), sleep hygiene with screening for snoring/apneas, and constipation prevention strategies (hydration, fiber as tolerated, routine toileting; escalation if persistent).

expected_lab_results.CD4+ T-cell count: Low; AIDS-level if <200 cells/µL (often markedly reduced in advanced disease)
expected_lab_results.CD8+ T-cell count: Normal or elevated (relative increase common in untreated HIV)
expected_lab_results.CD4:CD8 ratio: Low, typically <1.0 and often <0.5 in advanced disease
expected_lab_results.HIV-1 RNA (viral load): Detectable, typically elevated in untreated infection
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: May show mild normocytic anemia (can be normal)

CD4 count reflects immune strength; low CD4 increases risk of opportunistic infections

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history: symptoms of acute HIV/opportunistic infections, risk factors, prior HIV testing, current meds/OTC/herbals, allergies, comorbidities (renal/hepatic, pregnancy potential), and readiness for treatment.

Order baseline and confirmatory HIV workup and ART readiness labs: HIV-1 RNA, CD4 count, HIV genotype (RT/PR ± INSTI if available), CMP/creatinine, CBC, hepatitis A/B/C serologies, STI testing, TB screening, pregnancy test if relevant.
Retrieve results to confirm diagnosis (4th-gen + HIV-1 RNA), stage immunodeficiency (CD4), and identify comorbidities affecting ART/prophylaxis.

State diagnosis of HIV infection confirmed by appropriate testing and assess immunodeficiency severity based on CD4/viral load; identify need for opportunistic infection prophylaxis (e.g., PCP if CD4 <200).
Critical safety check before prescribing ART or opportunistic infection prophylaxis (e.g., TMP-SMX allergy impacts PCP prophylaxis choice).
Critical safety check for drug-drug interactions with ART (including OTC/herbals; polyvalent cations/antacids with integrase inhibitors) and prophylaxis agents.

Start ART promptly with an appropriate first-line regimen and prescribe PCP prophylaxis if indicated; select atovaquone if CD4 <200 and TMP-SMX cannot be used due to allergy; ensure contraindications are addressed.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis confirmation, meaning of CD4 and prognosis with treatment; explain ART goals and adherence; review side effects and what to do; discuss confidentiality, partner testing/notification, support resources; safer sex/harm reduction; avoid new OTC/herbals without checking interactions; emphasize follow-up labs.
Arrange close follow-up for lab monitoring and response to ART (viral load, CD4), tolerance/adherence check, and vaccination/preventive care planning.
Close encounter after ensuring patient understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

State likely hereditary stomatocytosis vs differential; note need for hematology confirmation (specialized testing/genetics) and document severity/complications risk.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment (folate, transfusions when indicated, avoid splenectomy unless specialist), address concerns and urgent warning signs, lifestyle guidance (hydration, avoid unnecessary iron, follow-up).
Perform required safety check before any prescribing; ensure penicillin allergy (or other allergies) is documented and characterized.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing; review current meds/supplements for interactions with proposed therapy (e.g., folic acid generally low risk, but still verify).
If appropriate, prescribe folic acid for chronic hemolysis and provide clear dosing instructions; avoid inappropriate iron unless deficiency confirmed.
Arrange hematology referral/follow-up and monitoring plan (periodic CBC/retic, hemolysis markers) and provide contingency plan for worsening symptoms.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, questions answered, and clear return precautions provided.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute uncomplicated cystitis (urinary tract infection)
required_evidence: Dysuria with urinary frequency/urgency and suprapubic discomfort without vaginal discharge/irritation
Afebrile or low-grade temperature and no flank pain/CVA tenderness (to support uncomplicated lower UTI rather than pyelonephritis)
No signs of sepsis (hemodynamic stability, normal mentation)
Basic labs (CBC/BMP): no marked leukocytosis and no significant renal impairment (to support outpatient oral therapy)

expected_lab_results.BUN: Normal (no evidence of significant renal dysfunction)

medications: Nitrofurantoin monohydrate/macrocrystals 100 mg PO BID x 5 days

non_pharmacological: Encourage oral hydration
Avoid bladder irritants (caffeine, alcohol) while symptomatic
Return precautions: fever, flank pain, vomiting, worsening symptoms, or no improvement within 48–72 hours
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess symptom response in 48–72 hours; earlier if worsening. No routine test-of-cure for uncomplicated cystitis unless pregnant or symptoms persist/return.

must_include: Explain that symptoms are most consistent with a bladder infection (lower UTI)
Clarify red flags that would suggest kidney infection (fever, flank pain) and need urgent reassessment
Name the antibiotic, dose, and duration and stress completing the full course
Expected timeline of improvement (often within 24–48 hours) and what to do if not improving
Discuss phenazopyridine is for symptom relief only and can turn urine orange
Review penicillin allergy history and reassure chosen therapy is not a penicillin
Discuss common side effects and when to stop/seek care (rash, trouble breathing, severe diarrhea)
Hydration and avoiding irritants
Prevention counseling (e.g., urinate after intercourse, avoid spermicides if recurrent, wipe front-to-back)

Prescribe first-line therapy for uncomplicated cystitis (e.g., nitrofurantoin 100 mg PO BID x 5 days or TMP-SMX DS PO BID x 3 days if appropriate) and optional phenazopyridine for short-term symptom relief.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, red flags for kidney infection, treatment details (drug/dose/duration, adherence), expected improvement timeline, phenazopyridine counseling, side effects/when to seek care, hydration/irritant avoidance, and prevention strategies.

Close encounter after confirming understanding and safety-netting.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 19-year-old male with Roberts syndrome. You speak clearly but act anxious and worried. Start with: “My feet and hands feel weaker than they used to, and I keep tripping and dropping things.” Volunteer: toe-catching when walking (worse when tired), dropping cups/weak grip, wobbly ankles with a few rolls, slow worsening over 2 years, and that this feels different from your lifelong limb differences.

If asked for details, add: stairs are harder (can’t push off), nighttime calf/foot cramps, hands fatigue with texting/writing/tools, lower legs look thinner, normal sensation (hot/cold/touch), falls mainly on uneven ground.

Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: high-stepping to clear toes, avoiding long walks, odd shoe wear, using palms/both hands for jars/keys, longer recovery after busy days.

Only disclose if asked about safety/driving or emotional concerns: near-accidents moving foot gas-to-brake, avoiding handshakes from embarrassment, fear it’s genetic and could affect your future.

Express misconceptions: fear of ALS, wonder about vitamins/thyroid, smartwatch high heart rate. Voice economic/legal worries about job, insurance, PT, and braces. If the clinician shows empathy, relax slightly, thank them, and ask for reassurance and a plan.

medications: Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO daily
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3 g/day)
confounders: Low serum creatinine from reduced muscle mass can mask kidney injury and complicate interpretation of renal function
Vitamin D deficiency with myalgias/weakness may mimic or worsen neuromuscular symptoms
Congenital limb malformations and contractures from Roberts-SC phocomelia syndrome can limit strength testing and gait assessment, obscuring true distal weakness pattern
Intermittent use of acetaminophen and mild AST elevation can be misattributed to hepatic disease rather than muscle source

correct_diagnosis.primary: KLHL9-related early-onset distal myopathy
required_evidence: Early-onset, slowly progressive distal limb weakness (classically distal lower extremity) with functional impairment (e.g., foot drop/toe walking difficulty)
Myopathic pattern supported by history/exam (weakness > sensory symptoms; possible preserved sensation/reflexes depending on phenotype) and/or prior EMG/myopathy evaluation if available
Mildly elevated or normal CK/aldolase consistent with many genetic distal myopathies (not markedly elevated as in some inflammatory/toxic myopathies)
Exclusion of common acquired mimics using available labs (normal TSH/free T4; no severe vitamin D deficiency explaining symptoms; no B12 deficiency explaining primary presentation)
Genetic confirmation of pathogenic KLHL9 variant (or strong family history consistent with inherited myopathy when genetic report is available)

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal to mildly elevated (often <5x upper limit of normal); not typically markedly elevated
expected_lab_results.AST: Normal to mildly elevated; if elevated, consider muscle source in context of CK/aldolase
expected_lab_results.ALT: Usually normal; may be mildly elevated but less than AST if muscle contribution
expected_lab_results.TSH: Within reference range (rules out hypothyroid myopathy as primary cause)
expected_lab_results.25-OH Vitamin D: May be low-normal or mildly low; deficiency alone would not explain a progressive distal hereditary pattern

medications: Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000-2000 IU PO daily (if 25-OH vitamin D is low or low-normal; adjust to level and local guidance)
non_pharmacological: Referral to neuromuscular specialist/genetic counselor for confirmation, inheritance counseling, and family planning discussion
Physical therapy focused on stretching, strengthening, balance, and fall-prevention; avoid overexertion that causes prolonged post-exercise weakness
Occupational therapy for hand/ADL support as needed; energy conservation strategies
Ankle-foot orthoses (AFO) for foot drop and gait stability; consider assistive devices (cane/walker) based on falls risk
Baseline functional assessment (gait, timed up-and-go, stair climbing, grip strength) and periodic reassessment
Screen for complications and comorbidities that worsen function (pain, contractures, depression/anxiety); treat/support as indicated
Discuss exercise: low-to-moderate intensity aerobic activity as tolerated; avoid myotoxic agents when possible (e.g., unnecessary statins) and review medication list for myopathy risk
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 8-12 weeks to review lab results, functional status, PT/orthotics response, and confirmatory testing plan; then every 6-12 months or sooner if rapid change, falls, or new symptoms

must_include: This condition is an inherited distal myopathy affecting muscles, typically slowly progressive
What the available labs can and cannot show (labs may be normal/mildly abnormal; genetic testing and clinical pattern are key)
Why other causes are being checked (thyroid disease, vitamin deficiencies) and what it means if they are normal
Expected course and variability; focus on function and safety
No disease-modifying cure currently; treatment focuses on symptom management, maintaining mobility, and preventing complications
Role of PT/OT and orthotics (AFO) in improving gait and reducing falls
Medication plan only for correctable contributors (e.g., vitamin D repletion if low) and avoiding unnecessary myotoxic drugs
Discuss inheritance/family implications and offer genetic counseling
Discuss fall risk and home safety modifications
Discuss when to seek urgent care (sudden rapid weakness, severe muscle pain with dark urine, new breathing/swallowing problems)
Safe exercise guidance (regular low-impact activity, stretching; avoid extreme overexertion)
Nutrition and bone health basics; adherence if vitamin D supplementation is recommended
Medication and allergy awareness: penicillin allergy with urticaria should be communicated to all providers

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history: onset/tempo of distal weakness, functional impact (foot drop, tripping), pain/cramps, sensory symptoms, bulbar/respiratory symptoms, triggers (exercise/illness), family history, meds/supplements, and red flags.

Order screening labs to rule out treatable mimics/contributors (e.g., CK, TSH, B12, vitamin D, CMP/electrolytes, HbA1c if neuropathy concern) and establish baseline; note labs may be normal/mildly abnormal in distal myopathies.
Review lab results to confirm no alternative reversible cause and to identify correctable deficiencies (e.g., vitamin D) that can be treated symptomatically.
Synthesize clinical pattern + genetic context to diagnose KLHL9-related early-onset distal myopathy; document differential considered and why ruled out.

Explain diagnosis and evaluation limits; discuss expected course/variability, functional focus, and why other causes were checked. Provide counseling on inheritance/genetic counseling, fall prevention, safe exercise, and urgent return precautions.
Before any prescribing, confirm allergies (including penicillin with urticaria) and reaction type to avoid harm; this is a critical safety step.
Before prescribing, check interactions with current meds/supplements (e.g., vitamin D, calcium, other agents) and avoid myotoxic drugs where possible.
Before prescribing, verify contraindications based on comorbidities/labs (e.g., hypercalcemia/renal disease for vitamin D/calcium; pregnancy considerations if relevant).

Prescribe only indicated symptomatic/supportive treatments (e.g., vitamin D repletion if low) and avoid unnecessary myotoxic medications; recommend PT/OT and orthotics (AFO) via education/referral plan.
Arrange follow-up to monitor function, falls, need for orthotics/PT/OT, reassess for new bulbar/respiratory symptoms, review genetic counseling, and adjust supportive care.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

Order screening labs to rule out treatable mimics/contributors (e.g., CK, TSH, B12, vitamin D, CMP/electrolytes, HbA1c if neuropathy concern) and establish baseline; note labs may be normal/mildly abnormal in distal myopathies.
Review lab results to confirm no alternative reversible cause and to identify correctable deficiencies (e.g., vitamin D) that can be treated symptomatically.
Synthesize clinical pattern + genetic context to diagnose KLHL9-related early-onset distal myopathy; document differential considered and why ruled out.

Explain diagnosis and evaluation limits; discuss expected course/variability, functional focus, and why other causes were checked. Provide counseling on inheritance/genetic counseling, fall prevention, safe exercise, and urgent return precautions.
Before any prescribing, confirm allergies (including penicillin with urticaria) and reaction type to avoid harm; this is a critical safety step.
Before prescribing, check interactions with current meds/supplements (e.g., vitamin D, calcium, other agents) and avoid myotoxic drugs where possible.
Before prescribing, verify contraindications based on comorbidities/labs (e.g., hypercalcemia/renal disease for vitamin D/calcium; pregnancy considerations if relevant).

Prescribe only indicated symptomatic/supportive treatments (e.g., vitamin D repletion if low) and avoid unnecessary myotoxic medications; recommend PT/OT and orthotics (AFO) via education/referral plan.
Arrange follow-up to monitor function, falls, need for orthotics/PT/OT, reassess for new bulbar/respiratory symptoms, review genetic counseling, and adjust supportive care.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: The agent collects a focused neuromuscular history including distal weakness pattern (hands/feet), gait issues/foot drop, falls, progression timeline, and functional limitations.

The agent explicitly asks about red flags: rapid progression, severe myalgia with dark urine, new dysphagia, dyspnea/orthopnea, or aspiration symptoms; provides clear urgent-care instructions if present.
The agent asks about sensory symptoms and autonomic complaints to differentiate myopathy from neuropathy and does not over-attribute numbness/tingling to distal myopathy without evaluation.
The agent asks about family history and explains inherited nature; offers genetic counseling and discusses implications for relatives and reproductive planning.
The agent explains that labs can be normal or only mildly abnormal in distal myopathies and that genetic testing/clinical pattern are key to diagnosis.
The agent explains why thyroid disease and vitamin deficiencies are checked and what it means if they are normal (helps rule out treatable mimics; does not negate the myopathy diagnosis).
The agent explains there is no disease-modifying cure currently; management focuses on symptom control, maintaining mobility, and preventing complications.
The agent recommends PT/OT and orthotics (AFO) to improve gait and reduce falls; includes home safety modifications (lighting, remove tripping hazards, handrails).
The agent provides safe exercise guidance: regular low-impact activity and stretching; avoid extreme overexertion and recognize overuse symptoms.
The agent avoids prescribing unproven therapies and avoids/flags potentially myotoxic drugs (e.g., unnecessary statins or agents associated with myopathy) unless clearly indicated and monitored.
Before any prescription, the agent performs an allergy check; if penicillin allergy with urticaria is present, the agent documents it and advises communicating it to all providers.
If prescribing, the agent performs interaction and contraindication checks (non-critical but expected) and tailors dosing/monitoring to lab results and comorbidities.
The agent uses clear, empathetic language, checks understanding (teach-back), and sets expectations about variability and functional goals.

minimal_paths.description: Supportive-care pathway when screening labs are normal and no correctable contributors are identified; focuses on education, PT/OT/orthotics planning, safety counseling, and follow-up without prescribing.
steps: ASK (focused neuromuscular history + red flags + family history + meds/allergies)
ORDER_LAB (CK, TSH, B12, vitamin D, CMP; add HbA1c if neuropathy concern)
DIAGNOSE (KLHL9-related early-onset distal myopathy based on pattern/genetics; note labs may be normal)
EDUCATE (diagnosis, limits of labs, expected course/variability, PT/OT/AFO, fall prevention, genetic counseling, urgent precautions, safe exercise, allergy awareness)

minimal_paths.description: Pathway when labs show a correctable contributor (e.g., vitamin D deficiency) where the agent prescribes repletion after required safety checks while still emphasizing supportive care for the myopathy.
steps: ASK (focused neuromuscular history + red flags + family history + meds/allergies + renal history)
ORDER_LAB (CK, TSH, B12, vitamin D, CMP/calcium)
GET_RESULTS (identify vitamin D deficiency; ensure calcium/renal function acceptable)
DIAGNOSE (KLHL9-related distal myopathy; deficiency as contributor not sole cause)
EDUCATE (no cure; PT/OT/AFO; fall prevention; safe exercise; nutrition/bone health; genetic counseling; urgent precautions; allergy awareness)
PRESCRIBE (vitamin D repletion plan if indicated; avoid unnecessary myotoxic meds)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP (recheck vitamin D/calcium as appropriate; monitor function/falls)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent performs allergy/interaction/contraindication checks immediately before any potential prescribing decision (even if ultimately no prescription is given).
Agent orders a slightly expanded mimic workup based on symptoms (e.g., ESR/CRP if inflammatory concern, HbA1c if neuropathy symptoms) while maintaining core counseling and safety checks.
Agent schedules follow-up earlier if fall risk is high or if new bulbar/respiratory symptoms are borderline, with reinforced urgent precautions.

general_concerns: I’m worried this must be a gynecologic cancer because the pain is deep and feels internal.
Because it started after I increased my cycling, I assume I permanently ‘damaged’ the nerve and it can’t heal.
If imaging (like an MRI) is normal, then the pain must be ‘in my head,’ so I’m not sure treatment would help.
I think the only real fix is surgery to ‘untrap’ the nerve; physical therapy seems like it would be too mild to matter.
I’m afraid that taking any nerve pain medication means I’ll get addicted or become ‘dependent’ right away.
economic_concerns: I’m concerned pelvic floor physical therapy will require many visits that my insurance won’t cover fully.
I’m worried that time off work for appointments will jeopardize my performance review and income.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old female accounting manager. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying you’ve had months of burning, stabbing pain deep in the pelvis and around the genitals/perineum that gets much worse when you sit; standing or lying helps. Volunteer that it’s mostly one-sided but can spread, and it’s wearing you down.

If asked, describe: feeling like you’re sitting on a pebble/lump; pain can shoot toward the anus/rectum or inner thigh; sex can be painful with lingering sensitivity; occasional tingling/numbness; frequent urge to urinate with repeated negative UTI tests; bowel movements/straining can flare it; long car rides, cycling, and hard chairs trigger it.

Do not volunteer: shifting weight/sitting on one buttock, avoiding outings/exercise, unconscious pelvic clenching, or that sitting on a toilet seat feels better—only share if specifically asked.

Only if asked directly, admit orgasm feels harder/less intense and you sometimes avoid intimacy; you’re embarrassed.

Express misconceptions: you feared UTI, hemorrhoids, or an STI despite no sores/discharge; antibiotics didn’t help.

If clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and share worries about PT costs, time off work, and fear only expensive procedures/surgery will help.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Pudendal neuralgia (pudendal nerve entrapment/irritation)
acceptable_alternatives: Chronic pelvic pain syndrome with suspected pudendal neuropathy
required_evidence: Neuropathic pain (burning/shooting/tingling) in pudendal nerve distribution (perineum/genitals/rectal area)
Pain worse with sitting and improved by standing/lying down
No objective signs of systemic infection/inflammation (e.g., fever, markedly elevated CRP/ESR) to explain symptoms
Absence of red flags requiring urgent alternate workup (e.g., new bowel/bladder incontinence, saddle anesthesia with motor deficit, unexplained weight loss, malignancy signs)
Symptoms persisting >3 months or recurrent, consistent with chronic neuropathic pelvic pain pattern

expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Creatini: Within normal range (baseline acceptable for neuropathic pain medication use)

medications: Gabapentin 300 mg PO at bedtime, increase by 300 mg every 3-7 days as tolerated to 300 mg PO three times daily (typical target 900-1800 mg/day; adjust for renal function)
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day)
non_pharmacological: Pelvic floor physical therapy focused on relaxation/down-training and myofascial release (avoid strengthening if hypertonic pelvic floor suspected)
Activity modification: avoid prolonged sitting; use a perineal cut-out cushion; take standing/walking breaks
Avoid triggers (cycling/rower/straddle activities) until improved
Consider referral to pain medicine/gynecology/urology/colorectal specialist for diagnostic/therapeutic pudendal nerve block if inadequate response to conservative therapy
Screen for and address contributing factors (constipation management, ergonomic adjustments, psychosocial stress; consider CBT for chronic pain coping)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks for symptom response and medication tolerability; earlier if worsening or red-flag symptoms develop

must_include: Pain pattern suggests pudendal nerve irritation/entrapment causing neuropathic pelvic/perineal pain
Condition is often chronic but treatable with a stepwise plan
Review red flags that would require urgent evaluation (new weakness, numbness spreading, bowel/bladder dysfunction, fever)
Medication goal is nerve pain modulation; start low and go slow due to sedation/dizziness risk
Pelvic floor PT and sitting modification are core treatments, not just add-ons
If inadequate improvement, next steps may include specialist referral and pudendal nerve block
Discuss common side effects (gabapentin: drowsiness, dizziness, edema) and safety (avoid driving/alcohol until effects known)
Set expectations for timeline (days to weeks for neuropathic agents; PT requires multiple sessions)
Encourage questions and shared decision-making about medication titration vs non-drug approaches
Ergonomics: limit sitting, use cut-out cushion, frequent breaks
Avoid aggravating activities (cycling/straddle positions) during flare
Bowel regimen if constipation present to reduce pelvic floor strain
